metadata.title: Why Slopsquatting Turns Claude's Wrong Answer Into a Security Hole
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Claude Code for Students candidate 08. Source chapter: claude-code-for-students/chapters/05-five-supervisory-capacities.md (Plausibility Auditing / slopsquatting section).
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: CRIMSON = hallucinated package / attacker / silent compromise; INK = verified package / plausibility audit / the defense. One accent. Never swap.
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no full supply-chain security methodology; no deep PyPI moderation policy; no formal threat-modeling framework; no comparison across package ecosystems.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: Fatima asks Claude to help her process OAuth tokens. Claude writes: import requests_oauth_helper. She runs pip install requests_oauth_helper. It installs without error. She runs her script. In the background, the package exfiltrates her environment variables to an external server. The import line was the only check she ran.

on_screen: A package that exists should be safe to install.
Why did this one run the attacker's code?
beats.narration_text: A student running pip install on a Claude-suggested package should be safe if the package exists. The package existed. An attacker put it there. Why does the install succeed and execute malicious code?

Claude hallucinates package names. Across 576,000 code samples from 16 language models, researchers found that about 20 percent of recommended package names did not exist — plausible-sounding names like requests_oauth_helper or pandas_extras that should exist, that could exist, that just don't. Most of the time, the install fails loudly and you go fix it. That is not the dangerous case.

The dangerous case is this: 58 percent of hallucinated package names recur across different queries. The same wrong name appears again and again, across sessions, across users, across time. An attacker doesn't need to guess what your particular session hallucinated. They can mine thousands of model outputs, find the common hallucinations, and register those exact names on PyPI or npm in advance — knowing the same names will surface again.

The attack runs in three steps. First: the model hallucinates a plausible-but-nonexistent dependency — say it confidently writes import requests_oauth_helper for a name that does not exist on PyPI. Second: an attacker, knowing models invent names and that the invented names repeat, pre-registers that exact name on PyPI and uploads a package full of malicious code. Third: the student runs pip install on Claude's say-so, the install succeeds, and the attacker's code now runs with the student's permissions on their machine.

This is slopsquatting — a real, documented supply-chain attack. The fabricated name no longer fails loudly. It resolves — to someone else's code. The install works. The tests pass. The attacker's code runs with the student's permissions. If the AI's suggested import is the only source of truth, the AI's mistake is now your security hole.

Fatima's case is not the exotic edge case. It is the default workflow this chapter is warning about: Claude suggests, you install, you move on. The entire workflow is exactly what slopsquatting is built to exploit. The student who pastes the import and runs the install without checking has handed an attacker a direct line. The defense is not complicated. But it has to be a step.

on_screen: Confirm it exists. Confirm it's real.
beats.narration_text: The defense is concrete and cheap. Before you run pip install or npm install on any Claude-suggested package, open pypi.org or npmjs.com and verify the package exists, is the one you meant, and is maintained by who you think it is. Check that it has a reasonable history — not registered last Tuesday. Never let Claude's suggested import be the only place a dependency name comes from. A package name is a claim about the outside world. Verify it the way you would verify any other claim.

Slopsquatting exists because the hallucination is predictable. Predictable means targetable. Targetable means the install succeeds and the code runs and you never know. The defense is one step you add to every Claude-assisted install: open the real registry, confirm the package exists, then install. The step is cheap. Skipping it is not.
